news_summary: Advancements in solar energy storage, new marine discoveries, and innovative carbon management strategies are driving positive environmental progress while communities adapt to climate impacts.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés dans le domaine du stockage de l'énergie solaire, les nouvelles découvertes en milieu marin et les stratégies innovantes de gestion du carbone contribuent à des avancées environnementales positives, tandis que les communautés s'adaptent aux effets du changement climatique.
news_summary_es: Los avances en el almacenamiento de energía solar, los nuevos descubrimientos marinos y las estrategias innovadoras de gestión del carbono están impulsando un progreso medioambiental positivo, al tiempo que las comunidades se adaptan a los efectos del cambio climático.

articles.title: Scientists “bottle the sun” with a liquid battery that stores solar energy
articles.summary: Scientists at UC Santa Barbara have created a remarkable new material that works like a “rechargeable solar battery,” storing sunlight inside tiny molecules and releasing it later as heat — even long after the sun goes down. Inspired by reversible changes found in DNA and photochromic sunglasses, the system captures solar energy without relying on bulky batteries or the electrical grid. The molecule can hold energy for years and packs more energy per kilogram than lithium-ion batteries.
articles.content:
As solar panels lose their ability to generate electricity after sunset, one major challenge remains for renewable energy: how to store solar power for use later, whether during cloudy weather or overnight.

Researchers at UC Santa Barbara believe they may have found an answer that avoids the need for massive battery systems or reliance on the electrical grid. Writing in the journal Science, Associate Professor Grace Han and her research team describe a new material capable of absorbing sunlight, storing that energy in chemical bonds, and later releasing it as heat whenever needed. The material is based on a modified organic molecule called pyrimidone and represents a new step forward in Molecular Solar Thermal (MOST) energy storage technology.

"The concept is reusable and recyclable," said Han Nguyen, a doctoral student in the Han Group and lead author of the study.

"Think of photochromic sunglasses. When you're inside, they're just clear lenses. You walk out into the sun, and they darken on their own. Come back inside, and the lenses become clear again," Nguyen continued. "That kind of reversible change is what we're interested in. Only instead of changing color, we want to use the same idea to store energy, release it when we need it, and then reuse the material over and over."

DNA-Inspired Solar Energy Storage

The scientists drew inspiration from an unexpected source while designing the molecule: DNA. The pyrimidone structure resembles a component found naturally in DNA that can reversibly change shape when exposed to ultraviolet light.

Using a synthetic version of that structure, the team engineered a molecule capable of repeatedly storing and releasing energy. To better understand why the molecule remained stable while holding energy for long periods, the researchers partnered with UCLA distinguished research professor Ken Houk. Computational modeling helped explain how the material could retain stored energy for years without significant loss.

"We prioritized a lightweight, compact molecule design," Nguyen said. "For this project, we cut everything we didn't need. Anything that was unnecessary, we removed to make the molecule as compact as possible."

A Reusable "Sun Battery"

Unlike standard solar panels that directly convert sunlight into electricity, this system stores energy chemically. The molecule behaves somewhat like a compressed spring. After absorbing sunlight, it shifts into a strained, high-energy form and stays in that state until activated.

When exposed to a trigger -- such as a small amount of heat or a catalyst -- the molecule snaps back into its original form, releasing the stored energy as heat.

"We typically describe it as a rechargeable solar battery," Nguyen said. "It stores sunlight, and it can be recharged."

The molecule also delivers impressive energy density. According to the researchers, it stores more than 1.6 megajoules of energy per kilogram. By comparison, a conventional lithium-ion battery stores roughly 0.9 MJ/kg. The new material also outperformed earlier generations of optical energy-storage switches.

New Material Can Boil Water Using Stored Sunlight

A key milestone for the team involved turning the molecule's high energy storage capacity into a practical demonstration. In experiments, the researchers showed that the material could release enough heat to boil water under ambient conditions, something that has been difficult to accomplish in this area of research.

"Boiling water is an energy-intensive process," Nguyen said. "The fact that we can boil water under ambient conditions is a big achievement."

The technology could eventually support a variety of real-world uses, including off-grid heating systems for camping or home water heating applications. Because the material dissolves in water, researchers say it may someday circulate through rooftop solar collectors during the day before being stored in tanks that release heat at night.

"With solar panels, you need an additional battery system to store the energy," said co-author Benjamin Baker, a doctoral student in the Han Lab. "With molecular solar thermal energy storage, the material itself is able to store that energy from sunlight."

The project received support from the Moore Inventor Fellowship, awarded to Han in 2025 to advance the development of these "rechargeable sun batteries."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in solar energy storage using a novel molecular battery that stores sunlight as chemical energy and releases it as heat on demand. This innovation addresses a major challenge in renewable energy—efficiently storing solar power for use after sunset or during cloudy conditions—without relying on bulky batteries or the electrical grid. The technology has broad implications for sustainable energy solutions with potential real-world applications like off-grid heating and water heating, benefiting society and the environment. The article provides detailed context about the scientific approach, energy density advantages, and practical demonstrations, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with substantial positive impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create a liquid battery that stores sunlight for years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Solar panels produce energy only when the sun is shining, making it hard to use solar power at night or on cloudy days. Storing this energy usually requires big batteries or connection to the electrical grid.

**What happened** – Researchers at UC Santa Barbara developed a new material that works like a rechargeable battery but stores sunlight inside tiny molecules. This liquid battery can keep energy for years and release it as heat whenever needed, even after the sun has gone down. The material is inspired by DNA and photochromic sunglasses, and it uses a special molecule called pyrimidone to store energy in chemical bonds.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because the molecule stores more energy per weight than regular lithium-ion batteries and can be reused many times. The team showed it could even boil water using only the stored sunlight’s heat, which is a big step for practical uses. Unlike usual solar panels, this system stores energy chemically, so it doesn’t need large batteries or an electrical grid.

**What's next step** – Scientists hope to develop this technology for real-world uses like heating water or buildings off the grid. The material could be used in rooftop solar collectors during the day and release heat at night. Ongoing research will focus on improving the system and finding ways to make it widely available.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new liquid battery stores sunlight safely for years and releases heat on demand, offering a new way to use solar energy even when the sun isn’t shining.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent une batterie liquide qui stocke la lumière du soleil pendant des années
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les panneaux solaires ne produisent de l'énergie que lorsque le soleil brille, ce qui rend difficile l'utilisation de l'énergie solaire la nuit ou par temps nuageux. Stocker cette énergie nécessite souvent de grosses batteries ou une connexion au réseau électrique.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université de Californie à Santa Barbara ont mis au point un nouveau matériau qui fonctionne comme une batterie rechargeable, mais qui stocke la lumière du soleil à l'intérieur de petites molécules. Cette batterie liquide peut conserver l'énergie pendant des années et la libérer sous forme de chaleur quand elle est nécessaire, même après le coucher du soleil. Le matériau s'inspire de l'ADN et des lunettes photochromiques, et utilise une molécule spéciale appelée pyrimidone pour stocker l'énergie dans des liaisons chimiques.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car la molécule stocke plus d'énergie par poids que les batteries lithium-ion classiques et peut être réutilisée plusieurs fois. L'équipe a même montré qu'elle pouvait faire bouillir de l'eau grâce à la chaleur de l'énergie solaire stockée, ce qui est une avancée majeure pour des utilisations pratiques. Contrairement aux panneaux solaires habituels, ce système stocke l'énergie chimiquement, sans besoin de grosses batteries ou du réseau électrique.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent développer cette technologie pour des usages concrets comme le chauffage de l'eau ou des bâtiments hors réseau. Le matériau pourrait être utilisé dans des capteurs solaires sur les toits pendant la journée et libérer la chaleur la nuit. Les recherches continueront pour améliorer le système et le rendre accessible au plus grand nombre.

**Une phrase clé** – Une nouvelle batterie liquide stocke la lumière du soleil en toute sécurité pendant des années et libère la chaleur à la demande, offrant une nouvelle façon d'utiliser l'énergie solaire même quand le soleil ne brille pas.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean una batería líquida que almacena la luz solar durante años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los paneles solares solo generan energía cuando brilla el sol, lo que dificulta usar la energía solar por la noche o en días nublados. Almacenar esta energía suele requerir baterías grandes o conexión a la red eléctrica.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de California en Santa Bárbara desarrollaron un nuevo material que funciona como una batería recargable, pero que almacena la luz solar dentro de pequeñas moléculas. Esta batería líquida puede guardar energía durante años y liberarla en forma de calor cuando se necesite, incluso después de que el sol se haya puesto. El material se inspira en el ADN y las gafas fotocromáticas, y utiliza una molécula especial llamada pirimidona para almacenar energía en enlaces químicos.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque la molécula almacena más energía por peso que las baterías de iones de litio y puede reutilizarse muchas veces. El equipo demostró que incluso puede hervir agua usando solo el calor de la luz solar almacenada, lo que es un gran avance para aplicaciones prácticas. A diferencia de los paneles solares comunes, este sistema almacena energía químicamente, sin necesidad de baterías grandes o red eléctrica.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan desarrollar esta tecnología para usos reales como calentar agua o edificios fuera de la red. El material podría usarse en colectores solares en los techos durante el día y liberar calor en la noche. La investigación continuará para mejorar el sistema y hacerlo accesible para todos.

**Una frase clave** – Una nueva batería líquida almacena la luz solar de forma segura durante años y libera calor cuando se necesita, ofreciendo una nueva forma de usar la energía solar incluso cuando el sol no está brillando.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, translucent liquid-filled bottle shaped like a stylized sun, suspended above gently steaming water, surrounded by softly swirling molecular structures inspired by DNA’s double helix, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft golden light.

articles.title: Giant squid discovery uncovers a hidden deep-sea world off Australia
articles.summary: Scientists exploring deep underwater canyons off the coast of Western Australia uncovered a hidden world packed with bizarre and elusive marine life — including signs of the legendary giant squid. By analyzing traces of DNA floating in seawater from depths exceeding 4 kilometers, researchers identified 226 species ranging from deep-diving whales to strange fish rarely or never seen in the region before. Some of the creatures may even be unknown to science.
articles.content:
Scientists have uncovered a remarkable variety of marine life hidden deep beneath the waters off Western Australia's Nyinggulu (Ningaloo) coast, including evidence of giant squid and several species that may be unknown to science.

The discovery comes from a Curtin University-led study that explored the Cape Range and Cloates submarine canyons, located about 1200 kilometers north of Perth. During the expedition, led by the Western Australian Museum aboard the Schmidt Ocean Institute's research vessel R/V Falkor, researchers collected more than 1,000 samples from depths reaching 4510 meters.

Instead of relying only on cameras or captured animals, the team used environmental DNA (eDNA), which consists of genetic traces naturally released by marine animals into seawater. By analyzing these tiny fragments of DNA, scientists were able to identify species living in the deep ocean without directly observing them.

Giant Squid and Rare Deep-Sea Species Detected

Among the most notable findings was evidence of the giant squid (Architeuthis dux), detected in six separate samples collected from both submarine canyons. Researchers also identified deep-diving whale species, including the Pygmy sperm whale (Kogia breviceps) and Cuvier's beaked whale (Ziphius cavirostris).

Giant squid are among the ocean's most mysterious animals. They can grow longer than a school bus (10 to 13 meters), weigh between 150 and 275 kilograms, and possess the largest eyes in the animal kingdom, reaching up to 30 centimeters across, roughly the size of a large pizza.

Overall, the study identified 226 species spanning 11 major animal groups, including squid, marine mammals, cnidarians, echinoderms, and unusual deep-sea fish.

Scientists also detected dozens of species never previously recorded in Western Australian waters. These included the sleeper shark (Somniosus sp.), the faceless cusk eel (Typhlonus nasus), and the slender snaggletooth (Rhadinesthes decimus).

Lead author Dr. Georgia Nester conducted the research during her PhD studies at Curtin University and now works at the Minderoo OceanOmics Centre at The University of Western Australia. She said the findings demonstrate how little scientists still know about Australia's deep-sea environments.

"Finding evidence of a giant squid really captures people's imagination, but it's just one part of a much bigger picture," Dr. Nester said.

"We found a large number of species that don't neatly match anything currently recorded, which doesn't automatically mean they're new to science, but it strongly suggests there is a vast amount of deep-sea biodiversity we're only just beginning to uncover."

How eDNA Is Transforming Ocean Exploration

WA Museum Head of Aquatic Zoology and Curator of Molluscs Dr. Lisa Kirkendale said there have only been two previous records of giant squid in Western Australia, with no confirmed sightings or specimens collected for more than 25 years.

"This is the first record of a giant squid detected off Western Australia's coast using eDNA protocols and the northernmost record of A. dux in the eastern Indian Ocean," Dr. Kirkendale said.

To carry out the research, Dr. Nester gathered water samples from the ocean surface to depths greater than 4 kilometers. The eDNA analysis was paired with genetic reference material from physical specimens collected by the remotely operated vehicle SuBastian.

Taxonomists identified the collected specimens, which are now stored permanently in the WA Museum's Collection and Research Facility to support future research.

"The WA Museum contributed expert identification of specimens from the expedition, supporting the development of a local curated genetic reference that strengthened the eDNA analyses," Dr. Kirkendale said.

Dr. Nester explained that eDNA is especially valuable for detecting fragile, fast-moving, or elusive marine species that may escape traditional nets and underwater cameras.

"These canyons are incredibly rich ecosystems and, until now, they've been largely unexplored because of the difficulty of working at such extreme depths," Dr. Nester said.

"With eDNA, a single water sample can tell us about hundreds of species at once."

"That means we can dramatically expand our understanding of deep-water environments in a way that simply hasn't been possible before."

Hidden Ecosystems Beneath the Indian Ocean

The research also showed that marine life changes significantly with ocean depth. Even neighboring canyons supported different ecosystems and distinct biological communities.

Senior author Associate Professor Zoe Richards from Curtin's School of Molecular and Life Sciences said the technology could dramatically improve how scientists study and protect deep-sea ecosystems.

"Deep-sea ecosystems are vast, remote and expensive to study, yet they face growing pressure from climate change, fishing and resource extraction," Associate Professor Richards said.

"Environmental DNA gives us a scalable, non-invasive way to build baseline knowledge of what lives there, which is essential for informed management and conservation.

"You can't protect what you don't know exists. The sheer number of discoveries, including megafauna, makes it clear that we still have so much to learn about what marine life lives in the Indian Ocean."

Dr. Nester said gaining a better understanding of deep-sea biodiversity could help improve marine park planning, environmental monitoring, and conservation efforts over time.

"By combining eDNA with conventional deep-sea survey techniques, we can build a far more complete picture of biodiversity, revealing species, ecosystems and ecological patterns that would otherwise remain hidden," she said.

"This kind of information is critical for marine park planning and management, because it gives us a much clearer picture of what species are present and how communities are structured across depth."

The fieldwork was supported by the Schmidt Ocean Institute and the Western Australian Museum. The project involved researchers from Curtin University, UWA, The Western Australian Museum, the Minderoo OceanOmics Centre at UWA, the University of Tasmania, and Research Connect Blue.

The study, titled "Environmental DNA Reveals Diverse and Depth-Stratified Biodiversity in East Indian Ocean Submarine Canyons," was published in the journal Environmental DNA.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery revealing a rich and previously hidden deep-sea ecosystem off Western Australia, including evidence of the elusive giant squid and many potentially unknown species. The use of environmental DNA (eDNA) technology represents a breakthrough in marine biology, enabling non-invasive, scalable biodiversity assessment. This discovery has broad implications for marine conservation, ecosystem understanding, and environmental management, benefiting society by informing protection of vulnerable deep-sea habitats.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover hidden deep-sea life off Western Australia using DNA traces
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long found it hard to study deep-sea life because the ocean is very deep and dark. Traditional methods like cameras or nets often miss rare or fast-moving animals, especially those living thousands of meters below the surface.

**What happened** – A team led by Curtin University explored underwater canyons off Western Australia’s coast, collecting water samples from depths over 4 kilometers. They used environmental DNA (eDNA), tiny genetic traces left by animals in the water, to identify 226 species, including the giant squid and whales. This method allowed them to detect species without seeing or catching them.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it reveals a rich and mostly unknown ecosystem deep beneath the ocean. Finding DNA of the giant squid, a rarely seen giant animal, shows how eDNA can help find elusive creatures. The team also found many species never recorded before in the area, suggesting there may be unknown animals still to discover. This knowledge helps scientists understand what lives in these deep places and how to protect them.

**What’s next step** – Researchers plan to combine eDNA with traditional surveys to learn more about deep-sea life. The new information will help improve marine park plans and conservation efforts, ensuring these fragile ecosystems receive better protection as human activities and climate change affect the oceans.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using DNA traces in seawater, scientists revealed a hidden world of deep-sea creatures off Western Australia, including the giant squid and many unknown species, opening new paths for ocean research and conservation.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent une vie marine profonde cachée au large de l’Australie-Occidentale grâce à l’ADN environnemental
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Étudier la vie marine profonde est difficile à cause de l’obscurité et de la profondeur des océans. Les méthodes traditionnelles comme les caméras ou les filets manquent souvent les animaux rares ou rapides, surtout à plusieurs kilomètres sous la surface.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par l’Université Curtin a exploré des canyons sous-marins au large de la côte ouest de l’Australie, en prélevant des échantillons d’eau à plus de 4 kilomètres de profondeur. Ils ont utilisé l’ADN environnemental (eADN), de minuscules traces génétiques laissées par les animaux dans l’eau, pour identifier 226 espèces, dont le calmar géant et des baleines. Cette méthode leur a permis de détecter des espèces sans les voir ni les capturer.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle révèle un écosystème riche et presque inconnu sous l’océan. Trouver de l’ADN du calmar géant, un animal rarement observé, montre comment l’eADN peut aider à repérer des créatures insaisissables. L’équipe a aussi découvert de nombreuses espèces jamais enregistrées auparavant dans la région, ce qui suggère qu’il pourrait y avoir encore des animaux inconnus. Ces connaissances aident les scientifiques à mieux comprendre ces milieux profonds et à les protéger.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de combiner l’eADN avec des méthodes classiques pour en apprendre davantage sur la vie marine profonde. Ces nouvelles données permettront d’améliorer les plans de parcs marins et la conservation, pour mieux protéger ces écosystèmes fragiles face aux activités humaines et au changement climatique.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Grâce à l’ADN trouvé dans l’eau de mer, les scientifiques ont révélé un monde caché de créatures marines profondes au large de l’Australie-Occidentale, y compris le calmar géant et de nombreuses espèces inconnues, ouvrant la voie à de nouvelles recherches et protections océaniques.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren vida marina profunda oculta frente a Australia Occidental usando ADN ambiental
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Estudiar la vida en las profundidades del mar es difícil debido a la oscuridad y profundidad del océano. Los métodos tradicionales como cámaras o redes suelen perder animales raros o rápidos, especialmente los que viven a varios kilómetros bajo la superficie.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo dirigido por la Universidad Curtin exploró cañones submarinos frente a la costa de Australia Occidental, tomando muestras de agua a más de 4 kilómetros de profundidad. Usaron ADN ambiental (eADN), pequeñas huellas genéticas que los animales dejan en el agua, para identificar 226 especies, incluyendo el calamar gigante y ballenas. Esto les permitió detectar especies sin verlas ni capturarlas.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra un ecosistema rico y casi desconocido en las profundidades del océano. Encontrar ADN del calamar gigante, un animal raro, demuestra cómo el eADN puede ayudar a encontrar criaturas difíciles de ver. También hallaron muchas especies nunca antes registradas en la zona, lo que sugiere que aún hay animales desconocidos. Este conocimiento ayuda a entender mejor estos lugares y a protegerlos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean combinar el eADN con métodos tradicionales para aprender más sobre la vida marina profunda. La nueva información ayudará a mejorar la planificación de parques marinos y esfuerzos de conservación para proteger estos ecosistemas frágiles frente a la actividad humana y el cambio climático.

**Frase clave** – Usando rastros de ADN en el agua de mar, científicos revelaron un mundo oculto de criaturas marinas profundas frente a Australia Occidental, incluyendo el calamar gigante y muchas especies desconocidas, abriendo nuevas vías para la investigación y conservación oceánica.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a mysterious deep-sea scene showing a giant squid gracefully extending its long tentacles through dark submarine canyons, surrounded by subtle silhouettes of deep-diving whales and rare, unusual fish species, with softly glowing fragments of DNA strands gently drifting in the water to symbolize environmental DNA revealing hidden marine life.

articles.title: Will burying dead trees after a wildfire keep their carbon locked up?
articles.summary: Partially burnt trees still standing after a wildfire are typically felled and burned, but a US start-up claims burying them instead will trap the carbon underground for centuries
articles.content:
Partially burnt trees still standing after a wildfire are typically felled and burned, but a US start-up claims burying them instead will trap the carbon underground for centuries

Charred trees still standing after a wildfire in Glacier National Park, Montana Gorski/NurPhoto/Shutters​tock

In 2021, a smouldering underground coal seam ignited the tinder-dry grass and brush in Poverty Flats, Montana, setting off a wildfire that burned 267 square kilometres. The blaze killed 50,000 trees, mostly ponderosa pines, that had shaded cattle grazing on the Gentry Ranch.

Black, partially burnt snags stood across a moonscape of charred earth. These “widowmakers” could have unpredictably toppled onto workers or cows, or fuelled the next wildfire. Standard practice would have been to burn the trees in piles, emitting almost 7000 tonnes of carbon dioxide.

Instead, bulldozers and logging machines with giant claws dumped the trees into a 5000-square-metre pit and covered them over with 6 meters of soil and gravel and a polypropylene fabric. The company running the operation, Mast Reforestation, says this way the trees won’t decompose for centuries, preventing more global warming and making another fire less likely. Mast can also sell carbon credits to pay for planting new trees.

“By no means do we consider this a silver bullet – it requires all of the other pathways that are out there – but for forests, this is a very, very strong tool,” says Mast CEO Grant Canary.

The United Nations climate body says to compensate for hard-to-abate emissions, humanity will need to remove CO2 from the atmosphere with techniques like planting trees, building air-filtering machines or adding alkalis to the ocean. Because plants and trees absorb CO2 when they grow and emit it after they die, startups have been charring forestry and agriculture waste to make biochar and spreading it on fields, compressing it into bricks to store underground, or turning it into bio-oil to inject into old oil wells.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Earth Edition Unmissable news about our planet, delivered straight to your inbox each month. Sign up to newsletter

Mast has expanded this to wood killed by wildfires, which are now burning 10 times more of the western US than 40 years ago. The west is bracing for another brutal fire season after a record heatwave led to a record-low snowpack this spring.

Up to 99 per cent of a tree’s mass can remain after it’s killed by fire – although the amount varies widely – and about half that mass is carbon. For the Gentry project, Mast weighed the dead trees on lorries at a weighbridge and calculated almost 7000 tonnes of CO2-equivalent mass based on nitrogen and moisture content. It subtracted the machinery’s CO2 emissions and an estimate of a small amount of potential tree decomposition to sell 4277 credits verified by the Puro.Earth registry, each representing 1 tonne of CO2 removal.

For large-volume purchases, a credit from Mast can cost under $200, more than a credit for planting trees, but near the price of a biochar credit.

Ponderosa pine seedlings were planted on an eastern Montana property burned by wildfire Mast Reforestation

Mast spent part of the profits to raise seedlings and plant them on the ranch, while saving the owners the high cost of pile burning. Montana alone holds 6.5 million tonnes of accessible fire-killed trees, according to Canary, who is preparing a second project and plans to bury 150,000 tonnes annually by 2030. “There’s a lot of tonnes to be put underground,” he says.

The Puro.Earth standard says buried biomass can sequester carbon for 100-plus years. In 2022, scientists dug up a cedar log east of Montreal, Canada, that had lost only 5 per cent of its carbon after being buried for 3775 years. But wood can start decaying in months if it’s buried in the wrong conditions.

The fungi and bacteria that decompose wood need oxygen, moisture and warmth to survive. If you can seal the wood off from oxygen in the air, you can kill off these organisms, says Ning Zeng at the University of Maryland, who found the log near Montreal. At least 1 metre of clay-rich soil can typically keep air out.

While soil in the western US is mostly rocky and doesn’t have as much clay as the eastern US, glacial tills or other impermeable soils can often still be found within a reasonable distance of burnt areas, according to Zeng. But we need more research on how well different conditions preserve wood, he says.

“I’m very happy to see those commercial projects being implemented, but how to do it right is a key question,” says Zeng. “The implementers may not know, nor can scientists right now give actually a very clear-cut answer.”

If buried trees accidentally decompose, a project could potentially increase emissions because it burns fuel to dig the vault and move the trees there. Excavation can also kill plants and release soil carbon, although keeping the topsoil intact and placing it back on top can reduce that disturbance, says Zeng.

In a wrongful termination lawsuit, a former employee accused Mast of exaggerating the value of carbon credits it was selling from reforestation projects that did not involve deadwood burial. Mast says the lawsuit has been resolved and has not affected its operations.
articles.reasoning: The article describes an innovative and scalable method to sequester carbon by burying wildfire-killed trees, potentially preventing large CO2 emissions and reducing wildfire risks. This approach has broad environmental significance as it addresses climate change mitigation and forest management on a large scale, with detailed context on implementation, scientific basis, and future plans.
articles.personality_title: Burying wildfire-killed trees to lock away carbon for centuries
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Wildfires in the western US kill millions of trees every year. Normally, partially burnt trees left standing are cut down and burned, releasing a lot of carbon dioxide into the air.

**What happened** – A US start-up named Mast Reforestation tried a new approach in Montana. After a big wildfire in 2021, instead of burning the dead trees, they buried them in a large pit covered with soil and fabric. This method aims to stop the trees from decomposing and releasing carbon for hundreds of years.

**Impact** – By burying the trees, Mast prevented nearly 7,000 tonnes of carbon dioxide from entering the atmosphere. This method also lowers the chance of new fires by removing dry, flammable wood. The company can sell carbon credits from this process, helping to pay for planting new trees. Scientists say that wood buried properly can keep its carbon locked away for thousands of years, but it requires the right soil and conditions.

**What's next step** – Mast plans to bury 150,000 tonnes of dead trees each year by 2030, expanding this method across wildfire-affected areas. More research is needed to find the best ways to keep the wood from decomposing underground and to reduce any damage caused by digging.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Burying wildfire-killed trees offers a promising way to store carbon safely underground while reducing wildfire risks and supporting forest recovery.
articles.personality_title_fr: Enterrer les arbres brûlés par les incendies pour stocker le carbone pendant des siècles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, des millions d'arbres meurent dans les incendies de forêt dans l'ouest des États-Unis. Habituellement, les arbres partiellement brûlés encore debout sont coupés puis brûlés, ce qui libère beaucoup de dioxyde de carbone dans l'air.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une start-up américaine appelée Mast Reforestation a testé une nouvelle méthode dans le Montana. Après un grand incendie en 2021, au lieu de brûler les arbres morts, ils les ont enterrés dans une grande fosse recouverte de terre et d'une toile spéciale. Cette méthode vise à empêcher la décomposition des arbres et la libération de carbone pendant des centaines d'années.

**Impact** – En enterrant les arbres, Mast a évité près de 7 000 tonnes de dioxyde de carbone dans l'atmosphère. Cette méthode réduit aussi le risque de nouveaux incendies en enlevant du bois sec et inflammable. L'entreprise peut vendre des crédits carbone issus de ce procédé, ce qui aide à financer la plantation de nouveaux arbres. Les scientifiques expliquent que le bois bien enterré peut garder son carbone pendant des milliers d'années, mais cela dépend des bonnes conditions de sol.

**Prochaine étape** – Mast prévoit d'enterrer 150 000 tonnes d'arbres morts chaque année d'ici 2030, en étendant cette méthode aux zones touchées par les incendies. Davantage de recherches sont nécessaires pour trouver les meilleures façons d'empêcher la décomposition du bois sous terre et de limiter les impacts du creusement.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Enterrer les arbres morts par les incendies offre une solution prometteuse pour stocker le carbone en toute sécurité sous terre tout en réduisant les risques d'incendie et en aidant à la régénération des forêts.
articles.personality_title_es: Enterrar árboles muertos por incendios para almacenar carbono durante siglos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, millones de árboles mueren en incendios forestales en el oeste de Estados Unidos. Normalmente, los árboles parcialmente quemados que quedan en pie se cortan y queman, liberando mucho dióxido de carbono al aire.

**Qué pasó** – Una empresa emergente estadounidense llamada Mast Reforestation probó un nuevo método en Montana. Después de un gran incendio en 2021, en lugar de quemar los árboles muertos, los enterraron en una gran fosa cubierta con tierra y una tela especial. Este método busca evitar que los árboles se descompongan y liberen carbono durante cientos de años.

**Impacto** – Al enterrar los árboles, Mast evitó casi 7,000 toneladas de dióxido de carbono en la atmósfera. Este método también reduce el riesgo de nuevos incendios al eliminar madera seca e inflamable. La empresa puede vender créditos de carbono de este proceso, ayudando a financiar la plantación de nuevos árboles. Los científicos dicen que la madera enterrada correctamente puede conservar su carbono por miles de años, pero necesita las condiciones adecuadas en el suelo.

**Próximo paso** – Mast planea enterrar 150,000 toneladas de árboles muertos cada año para 2030, expandiendo este método en áreas afectadas por incendios. Se necesita más investigación para encontrar las mejores formas de evitar la descomposición bajo tierra y reducir daños causados por la excavación.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Enterrar árboles muertos por incendios ofrece una forma prometedora de almacenar carbono de manera segura bajo tierra, reducir riesgos de incendios y apoyar la recuperación forestal.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vast charred forest landscape with blackened ponderosa pine snags being gently lowered by clawed machinery into a deep earth pit lined with layers of soil and a protective fabric, surrounded by newly sprouted pine seedlings glowing softly at the edges, symbolizing carbon being locked underground and new life emerging from wildfire recovery.

articles.title: How climate stress is reshaping the fishing sector in Barbados
articles.summary: The socioeconomic toll of climate change is steep, but young Fishery Biology students are helping collect data to bridge the gap in the management and conservation of marine ecosystems.
articles.content:
By Temeka Maxime, Simone Merchant, Dania Greaves, Martin Weekes, and Linton Arneaud

Caribbean fisheries are still recovering from the damage caused by Hurricane Beryl in 2024 and Hurricane Melissa in 2025 — yet, hurricanes are only one egg in the basket of uncertainty brought on by the climate crisis. Throughout the archipelago, vast sargassum mats stall boats, smother shorelines, and disrupt fishing activities, suggesting a tangible and inevitable impact upon various fisheries.

The region’s geography increases vulnerability to climate hazards and extreme events, the effects of which are mainly amplified by increasing global sea surface temperatures. This is the driving force that feeds tropical cyclones with exorbitant amounts of energy during development, resulting in the high level of destruction we have seen on many islands. When coupled with the influx of nutrients in the ocean from anthropogenic activity, it accelerates the growth and spread of sargassum mats.

At minimum concentrations, sargassum is an essential food source, refuge, nursery habitat and natural fish aggregating device for numerous marine species, including juvenile dolphinfish (Coryphaena hippurus) and the eggs and larvae of four-winged flying fish — but the current surplus has had detrimental effects on the marine ecosystem, reducing light penetration and productivity in coral reefs and seagrass meadows. These habitats are essential for fish populations in the Caribbean to thrive; their degradation directly affects the fisheries industry.

For the fishers of Barbados, the golden tide of sargassum is both a curse and an opportunity. Along the coast, it is a foul-smelling nuisance; in harbours, it causes vessel entrapments that clog engine intakes and destroy propellers. Yet, further out at sea, these same mats become floating gold mines, where young dolphinfish are easily — and perhaps too frequently — harvested from the safety of the weed.

Additionally, reduced landings for some species and, conversely, increased landings of juvenile dolphinfish that aggregate around sargassum constitute a practice that threatens not only dolphinfish stock dynamics but also the economic sustainability of the many who rely on Barbados’ fishing sector, which ranges from commercial to subsistence fisheries and is accessible and income-generating to all.

Infrastructure and resilience

The passage of Hurricane Beryl significantly impacted Barbados’ coastal assets and infrastructure, particularly at the Bridgetown Fisheries Complex, where over 200 fishing vessels and the breakwater jetties were damaged, approximately 2,500 people directly affected, and 70 boats lost.

To help alleviate some of the impacts of hurricanes, Barbados’ government put several measures in place to assist fisherfolk. The implementation of a Fisheries Recovery Plan provided business interruption benefits, helped repair and replace fishing vessels, rehabilitated marine-based parts of the Bridgetown Fisheries Complex and landing sites, and protected the coastline.

The World Bank Beryl Emergency Response and Recovery Project (BERRP), a USD 54 million initiative, provides direct financial aid to fisherfolk for boat repairs, and funds critical infrastructure upgrades at both Bridgetown Port and vulnerable coastal sites like Payne’s Bay. A standout feature of the six-year plan is the inclusion of a “climate resilient debt clause,” which allows the government to pause loan repayments during future disasters to focus on immediate relief.

In collaboration with the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) and the government of Japan, which provided USD 2.93 million in funding, Barbados’ Ministry of Environment, Natural Beautification and Fisheries also implemented a one-year Coastal Fisheries Resilience project. A Fisheries Benevolent Fund was created with a USD 500,000 donation from the Latin American Development Bank, alongside a 30-year revolving fund that assists fisherfolk in purchasing new boats, repairing existing ones, and settling any outstanding debts.

Despite these efforts, the socioeconomic impacts of Hurricane Beryl persist. Coupled with additional climate stressors such as rising temperatures and sargassum influxes, and with the instability of their livelihoods, fisherfolk have fewer incentives to refrain from harvesting juvenile dolphinfish. The competition to locate, harvest, and sell as much dolphinfish as possible has become paramount.

The initiatives driving change

Last year, two critical Acts were passed in parliament: the Barbados Sustainable Fisheries Management and Development Act and the Seafood Markets and Businesses Act. Provisions in the legislation promote the purchase of fish and fish products exclusively from licensed vendors.

The Act also highlights safety standards for handling post-harvest stocks, prohibits unsustainable fishing practices, protects marine mammals and coastal ecosystems through the designation of no-trawl zones in shallow coastal areas, and bans the harvesting or causing injury to marine mammals. These measures serve as a continued commitment to the vision outlined in the Barbados Fisheries Policy 2023–2033, which underpins the goal of “improved up-to-date knowledge of the fisheries resources in Barbados’s Exclusive Economic Zone” — integral to both the local and regional dolphinfish stocks.

There is a critical need to effectively manage stock and harvest levels, but this is currently undermined by weak regional data collection. In collaboration with The University of the West Indies Cave Hill Campus and the Barbados Fisheries Division, young Fishery Biology students at the Department of Biological and Chemical Sciences are assisting the Fisheries Division in collecting morphometric and meristic data under the “Dolphinfish Wild Stock Resilience Project.” The collaboration comes at a critical time to bridge the data gap for the management and conservation of marine ecosystems.

Insights, solutions, and recommendations

Preliminary data from six site visits, measuring over 300 fish across two major landing sites, suggest concerns regarding the increasing prevalence of smaller dolphinfish within the Barbados fishery. Based on collected weight and length data, approximately 46 percent of the catch fell below the five-pound juvenile benchmark. In Oistins, the fishing town on the island’s southern coast known for its famous Friday Night Fish Fry, most dolphinfish landed were undersized, despite only one site visit being conducted there.

Although the data do not establish climate change as a direct cause, they are consistent with broader concerns that changing ocean conditions and shifting fishing patterns may be increasing pressure on fisherfolk to maintain their livelihoods by catching smaller fish.

The implementation of pre- and post-disaster plans and an early warning and monitoring system for potentially intense hazards is essential to ensuring safety at sea under variable conditions. The Fisheries Division has publicised vessel relocation protocols and plans for disaster response, which were tested during one tabletop and two simulation exercises.

Incorporating fisheries insurance also improves resilience and recovery efforts for fisherfolk in the event of a disaster, allowing them to return to sea with minimal delays and reducing economic hardships, while still maintaining their income. A vessel insurance scheme is being developed in collaboration with the General Insurance Association of Barbados, especially as parametric insurance has already proved successful in countries such as St. Lucia and Grenada through the Caribbean Oceans and Aquaculture Sustainability Facility (COAST).

Raising public awareness about the long-term consequences of harvesting juvenile dolphinfish will go a long way in educating people and fostering a sustainable fishery for future generations. This increased understanding is essential to ensuring responsible fishing practices and preserving dolphinfish populations over time. The Barbados Fisheries Mock Trial Simulation provides an excellent example of how public awareness can drive cultural change, bridging the gap between complex legal theory and real-world application, and ensuring that stakeholders truly understand the regulations protecting Barbados’ marine resources. By prioritising education over incarceration, it empowers the fishing community to become active partners in sustainable conservation rather than just subjects of enforcement.

The Barbados Dolphinfish Tagging Project is a vital research framework that bridges the gap between public participation and international science by integrating citizens into a global tagging network. By crowdsourcing data on life-history parameters such as migration patterns and growth rates, the project generates the localised evidence needed to transition from general estimates to precise, data-driven management policies tailored to Barbados’ territorial waters.

Although the risks of rising temperatures cannot be mitigated, ecological managers can become more adaptable to reduce the effects on both fisheries and livelihoods, and long-term sustainability goals can be achieved through improved legislation.

Mechanised, large-scale sargassum influx removal offers a vital long-term management strategy; yet, its success hinges on strict alignment with existing regional mitigation frameworks projects. To safeguard biodiversity, fishery biologists must designate specific harvest zones based on evidence-led recommendations that prioritise critical habitats and species protection. Integrating climate-proof infrastructure at landing sites to protect the surrounding docks and boats during storms and other hazards can also reduce damage and high post-relief costs.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights significant climate-related challenges impacting Barbados' fisheries and details comprehensive, multi-stakeholder efforts including government policies, financial aid, scientific research, and community engagement to promote sustainable fisheries management and resilience. These initiatives have broad societal and environmental benefits, addressing climate adaptation, marine ecosystem conservation, and economic sustainability for a vital sector.
articles.personality_title: Barbados strengthens fishing sector with new laws and science amid climate challenges
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Barbados’ fishing industry is facing big challenges from climate change, including stronger hurricanes and large mats of seaweed called sargassum. These problems harm fish habitats and make fishing harder and less safe.

**What happened** – After hurricanes in 2024 and 2025 damaged boats and docks, Barbados’ government launched plans to help fishers recover. They passed new laws to protect fish and marine life, set safe fishing rules, and require fish to be sold only by licensed sellers. Young university students are helping collect important data about fish populations to guide better management.

**Impact** – These efforts are important because they protect fish like dolphinfish, which are being caught too young, threatening future catches. The government’s support helps fishers fix boats and keep working despite storms. Using science and new rules together helps keep the sea life and fishing jobs healthy.

**What's next step** – Barbados will continue improving its laws, collect more data on fish, and build stronger harbors to protect against storms. Projects like fish tagging and insurance for fishers will help manage risks and ensure fishing stays sustainable.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Barbados is combining new laws, science, and community support to protect its fishing industry and marine life against climate change challenges.
articles.personality_title_fr: Barbade renforce sa pêche avec de nouvelles lois et la science face aux défis climatiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'industrie de la pêche à la Barbade fait face à de grands défis liés au changement climatique, comme des ouragans plus puissants et de grandes nappes d’algues appelées sargasses. Ces problèmes endommagent les habitats marins et compliquent la pêche.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Après les ouragans de 2024 et 2025 qui ont détruit bateaux et quais, le gouvernement barbadien a lancé des plans pour aider les pêcheurs à se remettre. De nouvelles lois ont été adoptées pour protéger les poissons et la vie marine, fixer des règles de pêche sûres et exiger que le poisson soit vendu uniquement par des vendeurs agréés. De jeunes étudiants universitaires aident à collecter des données importantes sur les populations de poissons pour mieux gérer la pêche.

**Impact** – Ces efforts sont importants car ils protègent des poissons comme le dorado, souvent pêchés trop jeunes, ce qui menace les captures futures. Le soutien du gouvernement aide les pêcheurs à réparer leurs bateaux et à continuer malgré les tempêtes. L’association de la science et des règles aide à préserver la vie marine et les emplois.

**Prochaine étape** – La Barbade continuera d’améliorer ses lois, de collecter plus de données sur les poissons et de renforcer ses ports pour mieux résister aux tempêtes. Des projets comme le marquage des poissons et l’assurance pour les pêcheurs aideront à gérer les risques et à assurer une pêche durable.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La Barbade combine lois, science et soutien communautaire pour protéger sa pêche et la vie marine face aux défis du changement climatique.
articles.personality_title_es: Barbados fortalece la pesca con nuevas leyes y ciencia frente a retos climáticos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La industria pesquera de Barbados enfrenta grandes desafíos por el cambio climático, como huracanes más fuertes y grandes matas de algas llamadas sargazo. Estos problemas dañan los hábitats marinos y dificultan la pesca.

**Qué pasó** – Tras los huracanes de 2024 y 2025 que dañaron barcos y muelles, el gobierno de Barbados lanzó planes para ayudar a los pescadores a recuperarse. Se aprobaron nuevas leyes para proteger los peces y la vida marina, establecer reglas de pesca seguras y exigir que el pescado se venda solo por vendedores autorizados. Jóvenes estudiantes universitarios ayudan a recopilar datos importantes sobre las poblaciones de peces para mejorar la gestión pesquera.

**Impacto** – Estos esfuerzos son importantes porque protegen peces como el dorado, que están siendo capturados muy jóvenes, lo que amenaza las futuras capturas. El apoyo del gobierno ayuda a los pescadores a reparar sus barcos y seguir trabajando a pesar de las tormentas. Combinar la ciencia con nuevas reglas ayuda a mantener saludables la vida marina y los empleos.

**Próximos pasos** – Barbados seguirá mejorando sus leyes, recopilando más datos sobre peces y fortaleciendo sus puertos para protegerlos de tormentas. Proyectos como el marcado de peces y el seguro para pescadores ayudarán a gestionar riesgos y asegurar una pesca sostenible.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Barbados une leyes, ciencia y apoyo comunitario para proteger su pesca y la vida marina frente a los desafíos del cambio climático.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a serene Caribbean seascape showing a small fishing boat gently navigating through golden-brown floating sargassum mats, with vibrant juvenile dolphinfish swimming beneath the water’s surface near healthy coral reefs; in the background, a sturdy, climate-resilient harbor with repaired boats and protective breakwaters symbolizes recovery and sustainable fisheries management.
